content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 18, 1990


William H. Randolph
Chief Administrative Officer
County of Butte
25 County Center Drive
Oroville, CA 95965-3380

Re: Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-694

Dear Mr. Randolph:

This is in response to your November 5, 1990, letter
requesting advice concerning the conflict of interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Your request is one for
general assistance. Accordingly, we are treating it as a request
for informal assistance.²/

You have asked the Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") to review Butte County's proposed amendments to
their conflict of interest code prior to the public hearing.

A conflict of interest code is a document which lists the
positions within the agency that are in decision making capacities
and further provides disclosure categories which describe the
types of information to be reported by the employees whose
positions are designated in the code. The categories are designed
to require a designated employee's disclosure of the economic
interests which can be affected by decisions the designated
employee makes in his or her official capacity.

You would not designate in the code your county board of
supervisors (board supervisors are already covered under Section

_______________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-694
Page 2


87200 and have county-wide jurisdiction), nor would you designate
other county employees who are required to file under section
87200 and have been statutorily assigned full disclosure.

Section 87302 requires that a conflict of interest code
enumerate the positions designated, other than those specified in
Section 87200, which involve making or participating in making
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on a
financial interest. Each such designated employee is required to
file statements at specified times and disclose certain interests.

Pursuant to Section 82011, the code reviewing body for a
single county agency is its board of supervisors. All single city
agencies report to their city council. All multi-county
jurisdictional agencies fall under the authority of the
Commission.

The County of Butte is proposing an amendment to its conflict
of interest code. You have written to the Commission for
assistance and any advice or suggestions as to the accuracy of
your proposed amendments. As the Commission is not your agency's
code reviewing body, we are unable to assist you specifically in
the preparation of your conflict of interest code, nor can we
provide a detailed review of your list of designated positions and
whether they have been assigned appropriate disclosure
requirements. However, we are able to provide general assistance
in relation to areas of your code which may require correction and
provide suggestions to their solutions.

We have had an opportunity to review your county's proposed
amendments to its conflict of interest code and have the following
suggestions:

1) Based upon a cursory review of your appendix of
disclosure categories, we have the following comments and
suggestions:

Within each disclosure category you should remove
any language referring to applicable filing schedules. Removing
this language will prevent you from having to make unnecessary and
time consuming code amendments in the future. If you were to keep
this language in your code, you would be required to amend your
code each and every time that a statutory change were made
affecting the numbering sequence of the filing forms, i.e.,
Schedules A, B, C, ...etc.

It is commendable that you are providing descriptive filing
assistance to your designated positions. Perhaps, an alternative
would be to distribute a cover memo or factsheet to your
employees, listing the applicable filing schedules to their
specific disclosure categories. In this way, you would continue
to provide the assistance to your designees, yet in such a way as
not to cause unnecessary amendments to your conflict of interest
code.

Our File No. I-90-694
Page 3


     Disclosure Category VI, subparagraph 3, should be amended to
include the reporting of "income from sources".  Category VI,
subparagraph 3 should read:

     Investments and business positions in business entities or
nonprofit corporations and income from sources which are of
the type which provide consultant services to any business
entity or nonprofit corporation made reportable by this
disclosure category.

     2)    You have correctly designated the position of Chief
Investment Officer in your conflict of interest code.  At this
point in time, we are instructing agencies to continue including
filers who manage public investments in their conflict of interest
codes until such time as the Commission has clarified the scope of
application of the term "public officials who manage public
investments."  (Section 87200, factsheet enclosed.)

     I hope that the information contained in this letter is of
some help to you.  If I can be of any further assistance, please
do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel


     By:   Cheryl A. Hoff
           Staff Services Analyst
           Legal Division

SH:CAH:I90694

Enclosures

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 3, 1991

Mr. Mark Rynearson
Friends of Sparks
1715 Central Ave.
McKinleyville, CA  95521

Re:  Your Request Advice
     Our File No. A-90-695

Dear Mr. Rynearson:

     You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act.¹  This letter will confirm our
telephone conversation on November 21, 1990.

FACTS

     A group of individuals has provided funds for an ad in sup-
port of Anna Sparks for Supervisor.  The group is not controlled
by a candidate.  Individual payments were made to a newspaper by
each individual for the purchase of an ad on November 2, 1990.
The total amount paid for the ad exceeded $1,000.  The group has
filed a statement of organization (Form 410) as the Friends of
Sparks and a statement of termination (Form 415) and has provided
the filing officer with a copy of the checks used to make the
expenditure and a list of all contributors.

QUESTION

     Is the group a "committee" with filing obligations under the
Act and, if so, has the committee followed the correct procedure
for reporting receipts and expenditures?


_________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Mark Rynearson
A-90-595
Page 2


CONCLUSION

Based on the facts presented in your letter, the group
qualifies as a recipient committee and has incurred campaign
disclosure reporting obligations pursuant to the Act. In addition
to the statement of organization (Form 410) and the statement of
committee, contributions received and expenditures made by the committee
must be reported on a recipient committee campaign statement (Form
420, copy enclosed). In addition, the committee is required to
file a "late contribution report."


ANALYSIS

Under Government Code Section 82013, a recipient committee is
formed when a person or combination of persons directly or
indirectly receives contributions or makes expenditures or
contributions for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination
or election of one or more candidates, or the passage or defeat of
any measure. (Sections 82015 and 82025; Regulations 18215 and
18225 (copies enclosed).)

Two or more individuals must file as a single committee under
Section 82013(b) whenever individuals use their money to make
joint expenditures. (Madden Advice Letter, No. A-85-223, copy
enclosed.) The Friends of Sparks qualified as a committee on
November 2, 1990. Therefore, the committee's campaign disclosure
statement (Form 420) will be due by January 31, 1991, for the
period covering January 1, 1990, through December 31, 1990. Each
monetary contribution on Schedule A. Expenditures to the
newspaper should be reported on Schedule E. After completing the
appropriate schedules, the summary page of the campaign disclosure
statement must be completed.

In addition, Section 84203 (copy enclosed) requires that a
"late contribution report" be filed since contributions totaling
$1,000 or more to a single candidate were made during the period
covering October 21 through November 5. A late contribution
report is sent by telegram, mailgram, guaranteed overnight

Mark Rynearson
A-90-695
Page 3


delivery, or personal delivery and must be sent within 24 hours of
making the contributions. (See page 28 of the enclosed "Information Manual on Disclosure Provisions of the Political
Reform Act" for detailed information on when it is due.)  When it
after an election that a late contribution report was not filed,
the report may be filed by regular mail, rather than by telegram.
(Pessner Advice Letter, No. A-87-263, copy enclosed.)

If you have additional questions on this matter, please call
me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Sandra L. Taylor
    Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

sections.question_synthetic: The document addresses two separate matters: (1) whether Butte County's proposed amendments to its conflict of interest code are accurate and what general assistance can be provided, and (2) whether a group funding an ad for a supervisor candidate constitutes a 'committee' with filing obligations and if correct procedures were followed.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: For the conflict of interest code matter: The Commission cannot provide detailed review as it is not the code reviewing body, but provides general suggestions including removing filing schedule references from the code and amending disclosure category VI to include income from sources. For the campaign committee matter: The group appears to have formed a committee with filing obligations, and late contribution reports may be filed by certified mail rather than telegram if discovered after an election.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; Skipped (too short): conclusion; CONCLUSION has only 0 words; LLM: Document contains two separate advice letters merged together. First letter (I-90-694) is informal advice with minimal conclusion section. Second letter (A-90-695) has garbled/OCR-error text in the conclusion and analysis sections making full extraction difficult. Both letters signed by Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document addresses two separate matters: (1) whether Butte County's proposed amendments to its conflict of interest code are accurate and what general assistance can be provided, and (2) whether a group funding an ad for a supervisor candidate constitutes a 'committee' with filing obligations and if correct procedures were followed.

CONCLUSION: For the conflict of interest code matter: The Commission cannot provide detailed review as it is not the code reviewing body, but provides general suggestions including removing filing schedule references from the code and amending disclosure category VI to include income from sources. For the campaign committee matter: The group appears to have formed a committee with filing obligations, and late contribution reports may be filed by certified mail rather than telegram if discovered after an election.

embedding.summary: This document contains two FPPC advice letters: one providing informal assistance to Butte County regarding amendments to its conflict of interest code (Document I-90-694), and another regarding whether a group funding campaign advertisements constitutes a committee with filing obligations under the Political Reform Act (Document A-90-695).
